items.text: Ask about baseline cognition, activities of daily living, ambulation, assistive devices, continence, and recent functional change.
Ask about home setting, caregivers, available supports, medication access, food access, transportation, and barriers to care.
Ask about recent medication changes, sedatives, analgesics, alcohol, other substances, and adherence.
Ask about depression, anxiety, delirium symptoms, self-harm risk when relevant, abuse or neglect concerns, and immediate safety.
Ask about appetite, weight change, swallowing, hydration, sleep, fatigue, and symptoms limiting participation in care.
Ask about fever, pain, falls, trauma, bleeding, focal symptoms, and other acute changes that may explain decline.
Ask about suicidal thoughts, self-harm, homicidal thoughts, command hallucinations, access to lethal means, and ability to stay safe.
Ask about mood change, anxiety, psychosis, mania, sleep, appetite, trauma symptoms, and change from usual mental state.
Ask about chest pain, dyspnea, fever, pain, head injury, neurologic symptoms, pregnancy when relevant, and medication adverse effects.
Ask about psychiatric-medication adherence, recent changes, overdose or ingestion, alcohol, drugs, withdrawal risk, and prior psychiatric admissions.
Assess appearance, vital signs, hydration, pain behavior, and evidence of acute cardiorespiratory instability.
Assess alertness, attention, orientation, mood, thought process, agitation, and ability to participate in the examination.
Inspect oral cavity, mucous membranes, body habitus, skin integrity, bruising, edema, and pressure areas.
Assess bed mobility, transfers, strength, balance, gait, and assistive-device use only when safe.
Inspect and palpate symptomatic joints, spine, and muscles for tenderness, swelling, deformity, range-of-motion limitation, or injury.
Assess focal strength, sensation, coordination, tremor, and signs of withdrawal or intoxication when relevant.
Assess appearance, behavior, speech, mood, affect, thought process, thought content, perception, insight, judgment, and attention.
Assess vital signs, oxygenation, hydration, pain, glucose status if available at bedside, and signs of acute medical illness.
Assess pupils, tremor, diaphoresis, respiratory pattern, skin findings, level of consciousness, and gait only when safe.
Inspect for injury and assess focal neurologic findings, strength, sensation, and coordination when clinically indicated.
